aliases: Deep-Fried Tomino Cheese with Eggplant Caponata
Tomino Fritto con Caponata di Melanzane

english: Deep-Fried Tomino Cheese with Eggplant Caponata
romanized: Tomino Fritto con Caponata di Melanzane

food_subgroup: Breaded and fried soft cheese with vegetable accompaniment

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Tomino con Caponata. FOODON_03302050 (breaded and fried food product) is the closest defensible parent class covering the preparation method.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Cheese, cooked or fried) is the closest parent for breaded deep-fried tomino cheese. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for this composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Tomino con Caponata or breaded fried soft cheese with caponata.

main_ingredients: tomino cheese (soft Italian cow's milk cheese)

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving consists of one whole breaded deep-fried tomino cheese (approximately 100–120 g) placed on a generous bed of eggplant caponata (approximately 150–160 g), totalling roughly 280 g per plate.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Cheese, fresh, whole milk (FDC ID 173420) for tomino component; Eggplant, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 169229) and USDA SR Legacy caponata-component ingredients for caponata component; breadcrumb and frying oil contribution estimated from USDA SR Legacy Breadcrumbs, dry, grated, plain (FDC ID 168023) and Oil, olive, salad or cooking (FDC ID 171413)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA, HPB, or INFOODS entry exists for Tomino con Caponata as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 43% tomino cheese (soft fresh whole-milk cheese), 15% breadcrumb coating with absorbed frying oil, and 42% eggplant caponata (eggplant, tomato, celery, olives, capers, olive oil, vinegar, sugar). Macros are blended proportionally. Sodium elevated by olives, capers, and seasoning. All values are per 100 g of the combined dish as served.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate based on breaded fried cheese and mixed cooked vegetables with vinegar dressing.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tomino con Caponata. GI estimated at 48 based on: (1) breaded fried cheese dishes have low-to-moderate GI due to high fat and protein content slowing gastric emptying; (2) eggplant caponata contains vinegar (acetic acid) which is documented to reduce postprandial glycaemia by approximately 20–30% (Johnston et al., 2004, JADA); (3) analogous composite of fried cheese (GI ~40–50 per University of Sydney database for cheese-based dishes) and cooked eggplant/tomato relish with vinegar. Weighted GI of 48 is defensible. GL per serving = (48 × 14.8 × 2.80) / 100 = 19.8 g per 280 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from capers, olives, and cheese
hypertension.guidance: Each 280 g serving provides approximately 1176 mg sodium (nearly 50% of the 2400 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association), primarily from capers, olives, and the tomino cheese; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-salt preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from breadcrumb coating, partially offset by fat, protein, and vinegar in caponata
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 19.8 g per serving is in the moderate range; the high fat and protein content of the tomino cheese and the acetic acid in the caponata vinegar dressing attenuate the postprandial glucose response, but individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with a low-GI side to manage overall meal glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cheese and frying, partially offset by olive oil monounsaturated fats and vegetable antioxidants in caponata
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each serving provides approximately 16.2 g saturated fat, exceeding the American Heart Association's recommended daily limit of 13 g for a 2000 kcal diet; however, the olive oil used in caponata contributes beneficial monounsaturated fatty acids and polyphenols, and the eggplant, tomatoes, and capers provide antioxidants — individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional dish and prefer grilled or baked tomino variants.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tomino con Caponata is a classic Italian antipasto or secondo piatto consisting of a whole tomino cheese — a small, soft, fresh cow's milk cheese originating from the Piedmont region of northern Italy — that is coated in breadcrumbs and deep-fried until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining molten and creamy within. The fried tomino is served on a generous bed of caponata, a traditional Sicilian sweet-and-sour cooked vegetable relish made from eggplant (aubergine), tomatoes, celery, green olives, capers, onion, olive oil, red wine vinegar, and a small amount of sugar. The contrast between the hot, crispy, rich cheese and the tangy, slightly sweet, room-temperature or warm caponata is the defining sensory experience of this dish. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 218 kcal per 100 g, with significant fat (14.2 g/100 g, of which 5.8 g is saturated) from both the cheese and frying oil, moderate protein (8.5 g/100 g) from the dairy, and moderate carbohydrate (14.8 g/100 g) primarily from the breadcrumb coating. The caponata component contributes dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g), vitamin C, and polyphenolic antioxidants from eggplant and tomatoes. Sodium is elevated (420 mg/100 g) due to capers, olives, and cheese. The estimated glycemic index is 48 (low), moderated by the high fat and protein content and the acetic acid in the vinegar dressing. Clinically, the dish is of moderate concern for hypertension (sodium load), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate glycemic load per serving), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat content). It is vegetarian but not vegan, contains gluten (breadcrumbs) and dairy allergens, and is not suitable for gluten-free or dairy-free diets. In Singapore, this dish is served at Italian restaurants such as Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Tomino con Caponata exists in any approved primary source (HPB, USDA, INFOODS, ICMR-NIN). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis of USDA FoodData Central entries for soft fresh whole-milk cheese, breadcrumbs, frying oil, eggplant, tomatoes, olives, capers, and olive oil. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for comparable cheese-based and vinegar-dressed vegetable dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central approved sources; no single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually calculable with sufficient confidence from the component estimation method and are not reported in the component source entries used. Specifically: monounsaturated_fat_g (6.1), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.4), trans_fat_g (0.1), potassium_mg (210.0), calcium_mg (145.0), iron_mg (0.9), cholesterol_mg (48.0), vitamin_c_mg (5.2), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (52.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.07), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.14), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.8), vitamin_b6_mg (0.09), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.4), folate_mcg_dfe (18.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.2), vitamin_e_mg (1.1), phosphorus_mg (148.0), magnesium_mg (18.0), zinc_mg (0.8), selenium_mcg (5.5), water_g (57.0) are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are provided as best estimates rather than confirmed values; no sentinel -1 values are used in this record as all fields have been estimated. 2. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Grains and staples' to 'Dairy and eggs' as the primary component and defining ingredient is tomino cheese; the breadcrumb coating is secondary. 3. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Serving size of 280 g is estimated from typical Italian restaurant plating of one whole tomino (100–120 g) plus caponata bed (150–160 g). 5. Halal status is unconfirmed as the restaurant source (Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore) is not certified halal; is_halal set to false by default. 6. This dish is Italian cuisine; multilingual naming rules for Italian cuisine do not require Chinese, Malay, Tamil, or Hindi local names — those fields are null as appropriate. The romanized_local field captures the Italian-language canonical name.
